name: The Graces
description: The Graces, also known as the Charites (Χάριτες), are goddesses of charm, beauty, nature, human creativity, goodwill, and fertility. Traditionally, they are a group of three goddesses, often named Aglaea (Splendor), Euphrosyne (Mirth), and Thalia (Good Cheer). They are daughters of Zeus and either Eurynome, an Oceanid, or Eunomia, the goddess of law and order. The Graces are closely associated with the arts, beauty, and celebrations, often depicted as attending Aphrodite, the goddess of love and beauty, or accompanying Apollo, the god of music and poetry. They are symbols of elegance, refinement, and the joy of life, bestowing charm and grace upon mortals. Their presence brings delight and inspires creativity. The Graces are also associated with hospitality and generosity, representing the giving of gifts and kindness. They embody the ideal of balanced beauty and harmony, both in nature and in human society. They are revered for their ability to inspire joy and goodwill in all they touch, fostering positive relationships and social harmony. The Graces are often depicted dancing or holding hands, symbolizing their unity and the interconnectedness of beauty, joy, and generosity. Their influence extends to all aspects of life where beauty and grace are valued, from artistic creation to social interactions.
subtitle: Goddesses of Charm, Beauty, and Joy

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Graces
keyMyths.description: The Graces are born to Zeus, but their mother varies. They are the embodiment of beauty and grace.

keyMyths.title: Attendants of Aphrodite
keyMyths.description: The Graces are often depicted as attendants to Aphrodite, highlighting their association with beauty and love.

keyMyths.title: Dancing with the Gods
keyMyths.description: The Graces are known for their dancing and participation in divine celebrations, bringing joy and harmony.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Early Greek poem detailing the origins and genealogy of the gods.
sources.text: Early Greek poem detailing the origins and genealogy of the gods.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Epic poem that references the Graces in connection to beauty and divine favor.
